concept: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI
content:
Anxiety is always a future oriented emotion. We all have different fears, but the pattern of thinking behind Anxiety is the same for every single one of us. No matter what you are anxious about, all Anxiety follows this pattern:
AX = WI + AW + ICSI
Anxiety = What If? + Awfulizing + I Can't Stand It!
references: IB2: Awfulizing (Chapter 4); IB3: I Can't Stand It (Chapter 4); The ABCs of How Emotions are Created (Chapter 3)

content: 'What if' is projecting our mind into a future that doesn't exist and may never occur. The 'What If' component has to do with the story that you make up in your mind. 'What if my car doesn't start or I get in an accident?' 'What if my boyfriend dumps me?' 'What if my boss fires me?' This part creates at most a concern, until you add the other two elements. 'What If' statements are not a bad thing when taken by themselves. They can serve as a safety net to keep us from taking unnecessary and harmful risks. It's just problem solving. You only get the feeling of Anxiety when you add the next two parts of the equation.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; IB2: Awfulizing (Chapter 4); IB3: I Can't Stand It (Chapter 4)

concept: AW: Awfulizing — Its Role in Creating Anxiety
content: The Awfulizing happens when you take that event and alter it through your conception. 'If my car doesn't start or I get in an accident, it would be truly horrible and awful,' 'It would be terrible if my boyfriend didn't love me anymore!' or, 'If my boss fired me that would be catastrophic!' Every time you say that something is awful, you are taking something that is unpleasant or inconvenient to you and exaggerating the unpleasantness until it is so universally bad it simply shouldn't exist. Awful doesn't exist except in your mind—you created it by defining it as such.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; IB2: Awfulizing (Chapter 4)

concept: ICSI: I Can't Stand It — Its Role in Creating Anxiety
content: The final component, 'I can't stand it,' is the thought that is incorporated with the Awfulizing. Not only would it be terrible if any of the feared events happened, but if they did, you wouldn't be able to stand it. You would fall apart if you lost your job, your boyfriend or girlfriend, or if you were in an accident. You know this isn't true because you've withstood every other unpleasant thing that has happened up until this moment in time; you've lost other boyfriends or girlfriends, other jobs, and had accidents, but if you're reading this, you're still alive. This is immediately provable! Therefore, you have stood everything that has ever happened to you and you will until memento mori, the moment of death.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; IB3: I Can't Stand It (Chapter 4)

concept: Anxiety Is Always Future-Oriented — Key Distinction
content: Anxiety is always a future oriented emotion. We make stuff up about the future that doesn't exist and then we scare ourselves about what we just made up—very creative! When dealing with Anxiety, you are always looking for the 'What If' statement; it's what sets you up for Anxiety and panic attacks. Anxiety can be as mild as being afraid to approach the beautiful woman across the bar or as severe as consistent panic attacks. Fear has a unique power to prevent one from fulfilling their potential. It can influence practically everything a person does. It can destroy a man or woman of genius and suppress any talents they might have.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S (Chapter 5)

content: Now that you know what you are feeling, how do you stop feeling that way? This process can be broken down into three simple steps: confronting the thought process that causes Anxiety, then confronting the situation you believe is causing your Anxiety, and finally, letting it go. When dealing with Anxiety, you are always looking for the 'What If' statement. First, find and red-flag your 'What If' statements. Observing your inner dialogue is the first step towards changing your thinking. Also take note of what your body is telling you. What are you sensing and feeling when you start to feel anxious? If you are aware of your physical sensations, you can feel the Anxiety coming on even if you haven't found the 'What If' statement yet. This will trigger you to start looking at your thoughts and disputing them.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; The 4 Steps to Disputing (Chapter 4); Red-Flagging (Chapter 4)

concept: The Centering Breath — Technique for Panic and Anxiety
content:
If you are feeling a panic attack, do not try to dispute your thinking right away. Panic is by nature irrational and you will most likely make it worse by trying to battle it head-on. Don't fight the physical sensations. Allow them to happen while you turn your attention to your breathing. It is critically important that you practice the Centering Breath, a breathing technique to help you release negative emotions and physiological sensations. Forcing yourself to breathe deeply will 'hack' your body into feeling better.
1: Take a gentle breath in. Be aware of your inner dialogue and any tension in your body.
2: Pause briefly at the top of your breath.
3: Breathe out slowly while thinking, 'Let go.' You let go of two things; your inner dialogue and the physical tensions that you are feeling.
There is no magic number to count your breaths or time your practice, but the deeper you expand your lungs, the more you will relax, and the longer you do the exercise, the calmer and more centered you will feel. 'Let go' becomes your mantra, a phrase you can repeat to yourself as a cue to pause, breathe, and become still. With Anxiety, if you can use the Centering Breath to successfully dispute and let go of the 'What If' scenario that you are making up in your head, you won't actually need to dispute the Awfulizing or ICSI. You can simply be aware of the irrational beliefs as you let go.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; Training the Observer (Chapter 1); Why Zen Meditation is Essential Mind Training

concept: Confronting Your 'What If' — In Vivo Method
content: In order to conquer Anxiety, it is necessary to take risks. When you have completed confronting your thought processes, you have the tools and are ready to approach the very thing you are afraid of, to confront your 'What If.' You need to use your rational thinking skills and do whatever it is you're afraid of, whether it's speaking publicly, approaching an attractive person, flying on planes, getting in elevators, etc. It is important to face the very thing that you are afraid of. If not, your fears will follow you always. Whether your 'WI' is physical or mental, large or seemingly small, it will take more than one successful confrontation for you to fully overcome it.
references: Confronting Anxiety — Three-Step Process; The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI

content: We humans are all FHBs—Fallible Human Beings. We are imperfect; therefore logic and practical experience tells us we will make mistakes. Imperfect beings will perform inadequate actions that lead to unintended outcomes! There is no escaping that fact. No matter how hard you or I try, there is no way to live a mistake-free life. That's just the way it is, and it would be better to accept this fact or you'll find yourself in a never-ending cycle of Awfulizing and rating. Stop putting yourself down for making mistakes—you can't be any better than you are at any given moment! Conditions, remember? Instead, when faced with an undesirable outcome, ask yourself how you might do better next time.

Things to remember about mistake making:
1: If you are human, you will make mistakes.
2: Mistakes are opportunities to learn.
3: There is no shame in making mistakes.
4: There is no such thing as a mistake!
If we define a mistake as an unintended or undesirable outcome directly resulting from our behavior, then we humans are exceptionally talented at making mistakes. Mistakes, while often inconvenient to us, are not necessarily a pointless waste of time and energy. When you make a mistake you have an opportunity to learn, and if you've learned something, even the worst mistake was not without some benefit. Now that you better understand mistakes, let me let you in on a little secret—there are no mistakes! Mistakes are a product of conception, a subjective judgement of reality as you conceive it to be, not as it is. One of my favorite Zen proverbs states, 'Everything comes exactly as it does.' And so, if all the conditions are there for the event to have occurred, as it did, how could it be a mistake? It happened exactly as it should have happened!
references: IB5: Rating (Chapter 4); IB2: Awfulizing (Chapter 4); FHB — Fallible Human Beings; Resistance to Reality (Chapter 3)

content: A lot of people use the words 'Anxiety' and 'worry' interchangeably. Although the two are similar and often travel together, they are not the same. Anxiety is the name of the negative emotion—a feeling or belief identified by Awfulizing and ICSI statements. Worry, on the other hand, is a behavior you do when you feel Anxiety—it is a cycle of repetitive thinking in which you are actively feeding and entertaining those irrational beliefs. Worry is a covert behavior—no one knows you're doing it but you—and rarely has any benefit. Don't confuse 'worry' with 'concern,' the concept of caring about a matter that affects your welfare. Concern can be wise and efficient but worry almost never is! No one is born a worrier; it is a learned trait and habit that can be unlearned.
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; IB2: Awfulizing (Chapter 4); IB3: I Can't Stand It (Chapter 4)

content:
Some of the more obvious characteristics of worry that often differ from the process of Anxiety are:
• Preoccupation with and dwelling on the future event. 'I can't stop thinking about how my mother-in-law will berate me if this dinner isn't perfect.'
• Over-inclusiveness; seeing one event as innately connected to virtually all other phases of life. 'If I bomb this dinner party my family will never respect me.'
• Predicting that if a certain event takes place you will be an emotional prisoner and will no longer be in control of your own destiny. 'If my family doesn't respect me, my marriage will fail and I'll be alone!'
• Falsely perceiving worry as a positive trait to be deliberately cultivated. 'What kind of host would I be if I didn't worry about my guests being happy and satisfied with my cooking?'
• Assuming personal responsibility; fearing being labeled inadequate or being blamed for the outcome of an event. 'If I burn the turkey everyone will think I can't cook a simple meal and they'll be pissed at me for ruining their evening.'
• Focusing on and attending to only the negative elements of change. 'I hate having family over; it's too much pressure and I don't enjoy anyone's company.'
references: Worry vs. Anxiety; The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI

content:
The following program has been designed to minimize worry, but will only work if you follow it all the way through:
1: List as specifically as possible the predictions you are making and the negative consequences of each. Example: If the company folds (prediction), I'll be out of work (consequences).
2: Place a specific time frame around each prediction. When would it begin? How much time must pass? What conditions cause it to end? Example: If the company folds in one month, then I will have to go job hunting in one month.
3: List in detail the important characteristics of yourself and your life that will not change if the event takes place. It is important to recognize the essential stability of personality: even a highly-important event will not transform your entire life.
4: List possible positive changes that might occur if the event takes place. Pay particular attention to negative elements in your current situation and elements you've wanted to change even when things were going well.
5: List the feelings you anticipate having. Analyze each individually as if it had already occurred and dispute the irrational or untrue elements in each.
6: Actively imagine, for a period of about three minutes, being in the situation you're anxious about. Picture yourself in detail feeling calm and relaxed. Force this image until it comes clearly and easily.
7: Start planning and acting in preparation, even before the event happens. Having a contingency plan or plans is proven to be a productive way to reduce the stress associated with worry.
8: List actual or potential sources of comfort and support and how you might best make use of them. Be aware there are two kinds of support seeking; short-range escape and long-range assistance. Long-range assistance will provide lasting benefits far beyond the end of your feared crisis.
references: Worry vs. Anxiety; The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; Disputing — Definition (Chapter 4)

concept: Is It Important What Others Think — Framework
content: Yes, sometimes it is important what some other people think of you. It is certainly important that our spouse or lover thinks well of us. It is also important for our boss to think well of the work we do. But forget about your reputation, which is just another way of rating yourself. This is the opinion of others and you have no control of this. If you work to solve problems and are kind to others, most people will like you. So concentrate on that, not on yourself. Love people and use things. Let go of all self-rating and other-rating and you will at least be happy in this lifetime. Limiting our experiences and constricting our personalities because we are worried about what others may think is the opposite of living free. The irony is the fear of caring what other people think will not help your reputation or relationships; confidence inspires trust and ease, while neediness often leads to others around us feeling burdened. Nothing is more effective for our reputations than having positive emotions and a rational mind. Let go of the concepts of ego, pride, self-esteem, self-worth and respect, and you will have a ball.
references: IB5: Rating (Chapter 4); IB7: The Idea of Entitlement (Chapter 4); The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI

concept: Case Example — Elevator Phobia (Anxiety Formula with 4 Steps)
content:
A patient was terrified of elevators—just seeing the doors open was enough to send him into a panic. He couldn't even contemplate getting on an elevator and this fear was impacting his ability to travel, go to job interviews, shop or visit any friend in a high-rise building. Applying The 4 Steps to Disputing:
STEP 1: OBSERVE — The patient knew what he was feeling because he was having panic attacks doing something that most everyone else seemed to do without a problem.
STEP 2: SOS — That patient was thinking that the elevator might fall or that he might get stuck on it, and if it did, that would be awful, and he couldn't stand it.
STEP 3: IDENTIFY — When the patient compared his irrational beliefs to rational truth, he saw that elevator failures are extremely rare, laws governing elevator inspections are quite stringent, and there are a great number of safety measures and backups in place. The only rational conclusion was that the probability of his elevator falling at the exact moment he was in it was very small indeed. It is much more likely he would be involved in a car crash on his way to visit his friend in the high rise, but he wasn't.
STEP 4: REPLACE — Of course he would prefer that the elevator didn't crash. And the statistics are on his side—any elevator will most likely be fine every time he rides in it. The realistic chances of his dying in an elevator, for the rest of his life, are practically zero!
After about 10 to 12 months of weekly practice, combining rational thinking with in vivo (in life) desensitization exercises that involved going to many elevators in the Tampa Bay area, my patient can now ride an elevator to the top of the tallest building in New York without panic!
references: The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI; The 4 Steps to Disputing (Chapter 4); Confronting Your 'What If'

concept: Case Example — Open Field Phobia (In Vivo Desensitization)
content: A client was afraid of many things, one of the larger being open spaces. After several sessions in the office, it was time to confront his fears and they went to an open field. They parked near the center of the field and started walking distances from his car. They would get a little way from his car and he would crouch down, throw his arms out, and turn to make a beeline back to his automobile. He apologized and said that it felt like he was going to fly off the earth. He was told not to apologize, but to keep trying. They continued to progress one or two steps further from his car each time, and after two months he was able to walk all around the field without any trouble. Confronting your 'WI' may not be this dramatic or this obvious. For example, if you're afraid of public speaking, it might be as simple as giving a toast at the next big dinner you attend.
references: Confronting Your 'What If' — In Vivo Method; The Formula for Anxiety — AX = WI + AW + ICSI
